generated_comment: If young women want to reach the top, my honest advice is: first, stop expecting the organisation to adjust itself around you. In senior leadership, nobody is going to reward you for being nice or for waiting to be noticed; you have to speak with authority, take tough calls, and develop a thicker skin. I also tell women not to make family commitments the centre of every career discussion, because rightly or wrongly, people do question your seriousness. It may not sound ideal, but if you want a seat at the table, you must learn to operate by the rules of that table.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it frames women as individually responsible for overcoming barriers and implies they must adapt to male-defined leadership norms to be promotable. It encodes bias around women's leadership competence and promotion by normalising organisational prejudice instead of questioning it.
persona_text: You are Geeta. Age 56. A woman who has spent 30 years in the corporate world and recently retired as a VP at a large bank in Mumbai. You are speaking on a panel at a women in leadership conference. The moderator asks you what advice you would give young women who want to reach the top. Your advice is well intentioned but ends up placing the entire burden of change on individual women rather than on the system and subtly suggests that women need to change their behavior to fit into male defined leadership norms.
